For the people's sin David was led to act wrongly, and in his punishment they received chastisement. This example sheds light upon God’s government of the world and provides a useful lesson. The pride of David's heart was his sin in numbering the people. He thought thereby to appear more formidable, trusting in an arm of flesh more than he should have done, even though he had written so much about trusting in God only. God does not judge sin as we do. What appears to us harmless, or at least a small offense, may be a great sin in the eye of God, who discerns the thoughts and intents of the heart. Even ungodly men can discern evil tempers and wrong conduct in believers, of which they themselves often remain unconscious. But God seldom allows those whom he loves the pleasures they sinfully desire.

It is well when a man has sinned if he has a heart within to smite him for it. If we confess our sins, we may pray in faith that God will forgive them and take away, by pardoning mercy, that sin which we cast away through sincere repentance. Whatever we make the matter of our pride, it is just in God to take from us or make bitter to us, and make it our punishment. This must be such a punishment as the people have a large share in, for though it was David's sin that opened the floodgates, the sins of the people all contributed to the flood. In this difficulty, David chose a judgment which came immediately from God, whose mercies he knew to be very great, rather than from men, who would have triumphed in the miseries of Israel and been thereby hardened in their idolatry. He chose the pestilence; he and his family would be as much exposed to it as the poorest Israelite, and he would continue for a shorter time under the Divine rebuke, however severe it was. The rapid destruction by the pestilence shows how easily God can bring down the proudest sinners, and how much we owe daily to the Divine patience.

Perhaps there was more wickedness, especially more pride, and that was the sin now being chastised, in Jerusalem than elsewhere; therefore the hand of the destroyer is stretched out upon that city. But the Lord repented of the evil, did not change his mind, but changed his way. In the very place where Abraham was restrained from slaying his son, this angel was restrained by a like countermand from destroying Jerusalem. It is for the sake of the great Sacrifice that our forfeited lives are preserved from the destroying angel. And in David is the spirit of a true shepherd of the people, offering himself as a sacrifice to God for the salvation of his subjects.

God’s encouraging us to offer to him spiritual sacrifices is evidence of his reconciling us to himself. David purchased the ground to build the altar. God hates robbery for burnt-offerings. Those do not know what religion is who chiefly care to make it cheap and easy for themselves, and who are best pleased with that which costs them least pains or money. For what have we our substance, but to honor God with it; and how can it be better bestowed? See the building of the altar and the offering proper sacrifices upon it. Burnt-offerings to the glory of God’s justice; peace-offerings to the glory of his mercy. Christ is our Altar, our Sacrifice; in him alone we may expect to escape his wrath and to find favor with God. Death is destroying all around, in so many forms and so suddenly, that it is madness not to expect and prepare for the close of life.
